Stormy Weather at Black Beach This is a beach made of mining waste that they dumped into Lake Superior.

As a little kid I thought the black streaks in the sand of Lake Michigan beaches was “oil” (proving that I grew up in a post Exxon Valdez world). Turns out it harmless magnetite, which makes up 2-3% of all Lk Michigan sand, most of which is quartz with a bit of felspar.

Pieces of extinct corals and other Silurian marine fossils can be found on the gravelly beaches of Wisconsin along Lake Michigan. I selected some material from there today that I thought might do well in the tumbler. Update in a couple weeks! #rockhound #fossils

On board the Glass City Pearl with Lake Erie Waterkeepers and local leaders to view some of the beauty of our precious Lake Erie. Still much work to be done to protect this great natural asset for future generations.

Chondrichthyan-free lakes feature on t-shirts sold in every Michigan beach town. But the Michigan basin yields fossils of stem chondrichthyans from the Devonian seas that once covered the region, like this ischnacanthid jaw (UMMP VP 119596) and Machaeracanthus spine (UMMP VP 61686) #FossilFriday

C L A S S I C M O N O
- Black sea glass on black sand. The timeworn glass, smoothed by the waves over decades, was found in Catalonia and the black sand is from Lake Michigan. #ClassicMono #Monochrome #Time #AlphabetChallenge #SeaGlass #BlackSeaGlass #SeaGlassPebbles

Small potatoes, but it's amazing how many people find Paleozoic fossils in Great Lakes beach gravel & go "wow, I didn't know corals lived in freshwater!"
The fossils are older than the dinosaurs. The lakes are newer than Homo sapiens. Environments change. Also what .bsky.social said.

This weeks #Lego #FossilFriday is #Dunkleosteus In 1867, Jay Terrell, an amateur paleontologist, collected some fossils near Lake Erie. He donated them to the Ohio Geological Survey
In 1873, John Strong Newberry of the OGS described the material belonging to a new species Dinichthys herzeri. 1/2

Sodalite in the Great Lakes area can be found on all the Great Lakes. The are from the alkaline complexes in Canada via glaciation. There is one over Lake Superior, Lake Erie, and Lake Ontario. The alkaline complex in Arkansas has sodalite and I’ve collected about 100lbs down there.

.is.goblin.fyi Top Left: Agatized honeycomb fossil Top Right: Flower agate (I think) Bottom Left: Brecciated jasper Bottom Right: Granitoid I got these all at a Lake Michigan beach and I am looking forward to going out as soon as it's warm enough! ☺️ #rockhound #rocks #geology #agate

I “met” a wonderful person on X who I have maintained contact with for several years. Through my posts, she discovered a niece collects sea glass from the shores of Lake Erie and turns them into art. I just received six pounds of glass collected from the shores of Ireland and the Isle of Man. ❤️
A gift I received from Lake Ontario yesterday. It’s a fossil encircled by an iron deposit.

Day 4 #beadinthenewyear with Allegory Gallery .bsky.social Go out and find a piece of nature to use in your design today - since it was single digits this morning, I wired Lake Michigan beach glass that I found last year + pearls and AG Fine Pewter charms for a purse clip #jewelry
The granite boulder was on the beach on Grand Traverse Bay in Lake Michigan.

It's a form of iron slag that is found in only one place on earth, on the beaches of Michigan's Leelanau County, on the shores of Lake Michigan. It manifests in various shades of teal, blue-greens that vary as to which is those two colors dominates.
My niece Heather has found a treasure trove of sea glass along the shores of Lake Erie. She creates beautiful pictures for family and friends. She also has her own little cottage industry.
#lelandbluerocks #lelandblue from Leland Michigan it’s slag material found from an Lake Superior Iron Company that operated in the late 1800s #rockhound

Dropped off some goodies at The Mill in Manistique today. Some Valentines themed jewelry made from strawberry quartz, Czech glass, Leland Blue, Lake Superior Agate, and Sterling silver. The shadow boxes are a guilty pleasure 🖤.

This is one small section of my writing table. The stone figure in the center is a piece of sandstone I found along a Lake Superior beach last year. It looks to me like a cloaked woman bent in grief. Nature and art do their best to console us.

The focal is a material called Leland Blue glass. It comes from only one place, the shores of Lake Michigan in Leelanau County, a product of the Leland Iron Works in the the late 19th Century.

The metal detector is on the lead. Anyone that uses metal detectors have any options on the Bounty Hunter Quick Draw model? I have no idea what I'd be doing, but I am close to a lot of beach along lake Michigan.

Oh wow- I didn’t know it was a trade name. That’s what all the folks in my Lake Erie beachglass group call all glowy rocks. Good to know! And I’ll check out the map, because I’m on the eastern edge of CLE. 😁

New Layens hive set up in the apiary today. Ukrainian hive is leveled, baited, strapped down and metal tie wrapped to the iron stakes (Lake Erie winds and some bear protection). Hoping for a new swarm to make their home here this spring. We still have two more hives to set up over the next month

I miss combing the shores of Lake Erie for polished glass treasures. The purple one is amethyst...nice find. I have hundreds if pieces of Lake Erie glass so I get it. These two eggs are covered in small pieces of Lake Erie Beach glass...
No waterspouts at Lake Erie today but I did find some kind of fossil on the beach. I don’t know what it is but I’ll try to find out. I refuse to leave a chase empty-handed

I've been looking at cremation jewelry again and also at glass-- orbs or beads or sculpture or something. Keep wondering if there's anyone in Minnesota doing this. Looks like Lake Superior Art Glass does? I dunno. I want to do something, not sure what. lakesuperiorartglass.com/pages/custom...
Not so much a rock as industrial waste, but here are 2 examples of Leland Blue. It's a glass and metal slag from the Leland Foundry on the shore of Lake Superior.

The bubbles are just from the beach surf. This was found on Lake Huron. The cylinders are crinoid stems. They tend to fall apart pretty quickly into disks after the crinoid dies.

Since I’m being a whimsical bitch today I’ll also share these. Flowers and sea glass/shells/driftwood from the Great Lakes in little spice jars I’ve kept. I’m a glass jar goblin (much to my other half’s dismay).
Got my legs into Lake Michigan, found a sweet piece of beach glass

It's iron slag, impurities that separate in the smelting process, forming glass in these specific shades of blues with the flowering pinks [air bubbles resulting from overly fast cooling; given that they dumped the slag in Lake Michigan, you can see why].

One time when I was visiting .bsky.social we went to the beach on Lake Michigan and I got VERY excited abt these strange bubbly black rocks I kept finding, stuffed my pockets completely full of them, couldn't get enough, and then we got back home and I learned that it was slag 🥴

I know just enough about rocks and geology to be dangerous as they say. But I did walk the beaches along Lake Michigan and collected many pieces of quartz and beach glass and other interesting rocks tgat had been smoothed down by the wave action. Even found an occaisional agate.

Yup! That is coral! I'd find tons of that kind all over the place in lake Michigan and all over the ancient inland sea territory down the middle of the Americas. I have a piece laying around here somewhere that I carefully hand polished and kept in my pocket for years. I better find that...
Also found the same small business that made my favorite mushroom earrings and got a couple new pairs tonight. It’s wrapped sea glass from Lake Michigan.💜
Oooooh it might be a while before I can find some, but Lake Michigan has black iron oxide sand. It's magnetic!

Fairly sure these dark streaks in the sand along a Lake Michigan beach are made from “magnetite sand.” Do they segregate this way because they are of different density or shape from the other sand grains, or do they stick together because of magnetism

It's called Leland blue glass, a form of iron slag found only in the waters and beaches of the coast of Leelanau County, on Lake Michigan. The post explains in detail, as well as making the point that "Leelanau" is a colonizer's made-up faux-Native word, and the real identifier is "Leland."
Good morning… my talented niece collects sea glass along the shores of beautiful Lake Erie. Her work can be found on IG 🇨🇦❤️
When they are found in the UP, they are called Yooperlites, but I found mine on Lake Erie in Ohio. The same mineral, but it’s not the trade name. Lol They are all sodalite, however. 🤩

What's beautiful today? It's impossible to walk through the small creek that runs through our backyard without finding #fossils. We're in the Lake Erie watershed and, to my (decidedly un-expert) understanding, these fossils are mainly from the Denovian period. #rocks
Polishing some Lake Erie rocks up #rockhound #lakeerie #stones #rocks #lgbt #trans #ftm

Trinket jars are something I’m going to begin to offer at $5 a pop! These “seaglass” jars are up on my website ✨ These were all picked from the shores of Lake Superior from when I lived up there 🫶✨ #seaglass #trinkets #trinketsky #tinyjars #smallbiz #queerowned #mn #lakesuperior

An oil pyx for taking to hospitals for anointing got the MOST use. My ordaining bishop gave me a tiny antique box filled witch Lake Huron beach sand to sprinkle on graves (so often no dirt is accessible).
I'm always down to see some cool rocks. Reminds me of how my mother collects 'sea glass', glass that fell into Lake Ontario (or other bodies of water, I suppose) and were made smooth by it over time.

One day we could meet at the lake with our kids! Some of my best fossils were found by my eldest daughter! We also collect sea glass of course! One good group to join is on Facebook if you are on it. You can see what kind of fossils are local to our lakes:

Reflection in 100 year old glass at a cottage on Manitou Island in Lake Michigan.
one thing I missed about living near Lake Michigan was the chance to scour for sea glass

My people! Sadly our fridge wasn't magnetic. So we got a magnetic blackboard in the kitchen. Now, there's not much room for shopping lists, etc. Missing is a sailboat on water (Chicago/Lake Michigan) magnet that leaked all over the place & a puffy Freedom Tower magnet that I'm gluing back together.
When I was little walking the shore of Lake Michigan I used to find sea glass all the time. It's a testament to how we're not littering as much as I rarely find it anymore.

Leland Blue glass still washes up on the shores of that stretch of Lake Michigan: the only place it's found, making it a rare material. And it reminds us to seek, and to honor, the natural beauty that emerges from [human-caused] environmental catastrophe, a useful lesson now.

The furnace was sited right on the shores of Lake Michigan, in the town of Leland, in Leelanau County, hence the name of the slag. If you're interested in the history, and the vagaries of the names, I've written about it here [scroll to the latter section]: wingssilverwork.com/the-transfor....
Thanks! I’ve never found such a nice spherical piece like this before so I was stoked. Most of my beach glass has come from Lake Michigan so I’m happy to have some from out here now.

Beach glass hunting is one of my favorite parts of the beach. Where I live there were tons of glass factories right on lake Michigan during the industrial revolution. They dumped all the waste glass right into the lake for about 100 years so we have quite the abundance.

Resin boxes are sold out! The purple one was gone so fast that I was putting in payment information and it wouldn't let me anymore. Backed out and the only one left was the one Tim called floating on my back in lake Michigan, which I snagged. Appropriate as a Michigander Wood boxes still available!

Shipwreck Found at the Bottom of Lake Michigan After Nearly 150 Years www.nytimes.com/2026/02/18/u...
Ship wrecks intrigue me I have a ton of seaglass from when I lived on Lake Erie and have often wondered if any of the more interesting pieces could have been from dishesand glassware from shipwrecks

Yeah, that definitely looks like incinerator waste that saw some water erosion, but it could also still be industrial slag. Good quality slag glass is collected by some rock hounds. There is even a semi-precious stone made from iron ore/glass slag from Lake Michigan called Leland Blue.
This week's beach glass finds on Lake Erie.

In the old days before the US went insane, my wife and I would walk some beaches at the west end of Lake Erie in Western New York looking for beach glass. Same deal though. Items from Depression Era back to Victorian. I even made wire wrapped jewelry from it.
The Ohio Geological Survey really needs to join you! #LakeErie #GreatLakes #Ohio

That looks very much like vesicular basalt, but given that you found it around Lake Erie (and not the right location for vesicular basalt), I would say that is likely foam slag. The oxidation is a good indicator of that as well. Foam slag is a byproduct of the steel industry.

Look at this list of sets. Some sets on the left are subsets of sets on the right (“gold” is a subset of “gold or iron pyrite”), others are supersets (“water” is a superset of “water in Lake Erie”), and others overlap (something could be an oak tree, look like one from 10’, both, or neither)

Rediscovering rocks that I have collected, and now able to better identify them. I knew it was something and now confirmed a fossil trail, made by a Trillobite, walking on a once muddy lake bed, about 360 million years ago, Devonian period, found in New York, Lake Erie. So I think the skull was a woodchuck/ground hog. I’m totally unsure what the other pieces are. We were up north by Lake Erie and it was like bone island or something. I had to watch my kids closely near the water to really get to look but now I’m itching to go bone hunting again after this 🤣
It’s so pretty there. My niece was collecting sea glass today along the shores of Lake Erie. She creates beautiful pictures from her finds.

We have decent sea glass (mostly brown or green or clear) but we are on the windward side so we also have many bricks & all the driftwood.

Hell yeah it is. I live by Lake Superior, and sea glass is super common here, but finding it never gets old

Have you ever heard of Thompsonite ? Not sure if it’s a real mineral or if I have the spelling correct ! When I was just out of high school, friends and I traveled to a beach on Lake Superior that we were told was called Thompsonite beach which we picked up stones that we assumed was Thompsonite!

It's slag from the long-defunct Leland Iron Works, originally formed as the Leland Lake Superior Iron Company, an iron smelting furnace in operation in the late 19th Century in the upper northwest part of Michigan's Lower Peninsula.
Nothing like walking the shores of a body of water finding treasures. The north shores of Lake Superior are particularly wonderful for finding great rocks. Erie is beach glass galore.

Lake Huron Orgonite ✨Started as a way to use leftover gemstones and became a magickal creation. Made with resin, metal, crystals, beach sand, and shells, each piece is cleansed in the lake. Orgonite is believed to balance energy, reduce stress, and promote well-being. Have you ever felt its energy?

This is my newest magnet design. Made with glass, paint and sand from the shores of Lake Huron Canada. 1.1” diameter $5.00 each (shipping not included). I also do custom magnets. Ask for details if interested ❤️#art #magnet #seaturtles🐢 #turtle #handmade #sand #ontariocanada🇨🇦

When I was a kid my aunt and uncle had a cottage at Lake Huron. In the early evening we would go for a walk on the beach. I don't know exactly how many pretty rocks I collected from that beach, but it's more than 100. They're in the gardens at my parents place.

That's a good rock. Sorry I forget the geology name for it but around Lake Huron that type of rock is usually from an old geode which the lake wore into pieces - sometimes the little holes have tiny amethyst crystals inside, if you have a magnifying glass to check?
contractors found me a gorgeous blue "Ball Improved" mason jar in the ground behind my house (they said they used to dive for them in Lake Ontario)
Here ya go! Lighting is meh. Several fossils, lots of finds with cool bright reds & some jasper too. So much along Lake Ontario. I’m on the hunt for smooth labradorite.

🚂💎 Those “railroad marbles” found near tracks or Great Lakes beaches? They weren’t used by the railroad—they were used to make fiberglass! Shipped in barrels, some spilled and became today’s frosty beach finds. 🌊✨📸: 🔗 Learn more: bit.ly/rrmarbles
